title: Incorrect Data
text: The page presents the invalid data identified during the database creation process. Because the database enforces stringent constraints on the data structure, the data must be corrected before it can be imported into the database tables.

title: DB Schema
text: The page shows the database schema diagram along with table definitions based on the Mareano dataset.

title: DB Schema
text:
DB Schema Diagram
The proposed database design shown in the ER (Entity Relationship) diagram below contains five tables including three meta (entity) tables, one variable (look-up or reference) table, and one fact (measurement) table.
The columns with PK in the diagram indicate primary keys of the table, which guarantees unique identifications. All tables are connected by one-to-many relationships.

title: DB Schema
text:
Cruise Table
The cruise table includes Mareano cruise information and is a parent of the core table.

Cruise ID
The ID values in the cruise_id column are formulated as follows:

cruise_id: MA-{year}-{cruise_no}

title: DB Schema
text:
Core Table
The core table includes sediment core information and is a parent of the sample table.

Core ID
The ID values in the core_id column are formulated as follows:

core_id: {station_no}-{sampling_tool}-{core_name}

The {sampling_tool} part is three characters, padded with zeros, while the {core_name} part is two characters (or “00” if not available).

title: DB Schema
text:
Sample Table
The sample table includes specific sediment sample intervals and is a child of the core table.

Sample ID
The ID values in the sample_id column are formulated as follows:

sample_id: {core_name}-{depth_from}-{depth_to}

All parts are two characters, padded with zeros.

Note



The is_lld column indicates if the value is LLD (Lower Level of Detection). For example, if the original value is “<5”, the value is set to “5”, but is_lld is set to 1 (TRUE) instead of the default 0 (FALSE).

title: Pilot DB with Mareano
text: The site contains the initial version of the database design and its potential applications using the publicly available Mareano data set.

title: Pilot DB with Mareano
text:
Pipeline
The database will be a central part of the project to identify background sediment cores/samples. From the database, dedicated APIs should be implemented to maintain the data with both read and write access, while exported tabular files should be used for data exploration and the main data analysis. As the goal of the project, the result should be submitted to EFSA by exporting the identified segments in the EFSA format.

title: Pilot DB with Mareano
text:
Source Data

Chemical data page at Mareano: Chemical data: NGU and IMR

From the downloaded ods file, the data from INFO and INORGANIC sheets were used to create the pilot database.

title: Pilot DB with Mareano
text:
Database Design and Creation
After creating the ER (Entity Relationship) diagram, the tables were created in a single SQLite file with five tables. All required data were extracted from the Mareano tabular file and imported to the associated tables.

See the DB Schema page for details of the DB architecture.
See the Incorrect Data for issues found in the Mareano data set.

title: Pilot DB with Mareano
text:
Distances to the Nearest Coastline
To make the core/sample selection easy and effective, distances to the nearest Norwegian coastlines were calculated for the Mareano data.

See the Distance to Coast page for the result and methods for the distance calculation.

title: Pilot DB with Mareano
text:
Extracted Tabular File
A single tabular format file (.tsv.gz) was extracted and merged from all DB tables for the initial data exploration.

See the Data Export for the description of the file and for downloading.

title: Data Export
text: The data file was created for the initial analysis using the Mareano data.

title: Data Export
text:
Data Availability
A tab-delimited file containing all major data from the database is available for download at the following location.

pilot_mareano.tsv.gz

title: Data Export
text:
Data File Summary
The file pilot_mareano.tsv.gz is a denormalized (flat) export of the relational database, designed to facilitate immediate analysis without requiring complex SQL joins.
Each row represents a single chemical measurement (e.g., Arsenic, Lead, TOC) for a specific sediment sample. To aid analysis, the following data has been pre-joined to every row:

Metadata: Cruise information, core locations (including distance to coast), and sample depths.
Granulometry: The grain size distribution (Clay, Silt, Sand, Gravel) for the sample has been pivoted from rows into columns.

title: Distance to Coast
text:
As the initial data analysis will be performed on the Mareano cores/samples located within Norwegian fjords and surrounding areas near the Norwegian coastline, the distances to the nearest coastline were calculated.
The distance data is available in the dist_to_coast column of the Core table.

title: Distance to Coast
text:
Sediment Core Locations

Locations with Calculated Distances



Count of the Four Groups Separated by Distances






Distance
# Sediment Cores




0 to 10km
70


10 to 30km
26


30 to 100km
122


≥100km
165

title: Distance to Coast
text:
Methods
Coastline data were derived from the Global Self-consistent, Hierarchical, High-resolution Geography (GSHHG) database. High-resolution shoreline polygons were extracted for the region covering the Norwegian mainland and the Svalbard archipelago. The coastline geometry was cleaned and filtered to ensure topological validity before calculation.
To ensure accurate measurements at high latitudes, where standard global projections (like Mercator) distort distances, all spatial data were projected to the ETRS89-extended Lambert Azimuthal Equal Area projection (EPSG:3035). This Coordinate Reference System is the standard for pan-European statistical mapping and minimizes distortion across the region. The shortest Euclidean distance from each sampling (core) site to the nearest Norwegian coastline geometry was then calculated in meters.
The calculation was performed using the sf library in R.
